very much- We're competitive... in the median point in the OECD around our corporate tax rate. I'd like to do a quick sup. Yeah, a quick sup. So the real measure of success for Investment Boost is not necessarily the bringing forward of capital spend, because then you'll just have a peak and then it'll drop off. Yeah. The actual real criteria for Investment Boost being a success is the additionality.

Mm-hmm. Mm. What is the mechanism that you have to measure the additionality of spending as a result of Investment Boost, given it won't be till April, but is there a mechanism the IRD is doing as post-evaluation? The reality is I don't think there's any easy way to do what you're suggesting.

Ideally, from an assessment perspective, that's ideally what you would do. You would either have a control group or you'd have some base measure, but we don't have the luxury of either of those two. So it'll actually be very difficult. But I think international evidence does demonstrate that partial expensing does result ultimately in faster GDP growth.

So with a combination of what we'll gather through the IR10s and looking at the broader macroeconomic changes, which you would expect to see that over a period of a number of years. Those two will suggest an outcome, but it'll be almost impossible to measure the additionality, because we don't know what the counterfactual would've been.

Because Treasury obviously estimate it to be 0.1% per annum over the next forecast periods. Yeah. And the rest of it is fiscal drag, and- So I think the international evidence was something along the lines of over 20 years, it results in an increase in the capital stock of something like 1.6%. That's over 20 years.

The bulk of that apparently occurs in the first five. Mm. But actually measuring that is very difficult because it's not like we capture information about investment intentions from all of the parties for the next 5 to 10 years. So we don't know, we can't tell you explicitly how it's changed. All we can do is measure what's happening and try and infer from that what differences it's making.

One of the deliberate points around when the policy was designed was for it to be applicable, in effect, in a broad base. It applies to everything with very few exceptions. And the counterfactual to that policy from a simplicity point of view equally is, well, do you have a point of comparative basis of an aspect or a part of the economy that's not under that model?

Yeah, exactly. And that is just the trade-off element here. But we do know, and other jurisdictions have heavily utilized this as a mechanism, particularly through COVID. You are right, absolutely, that the purpose of this is around increasing capital investment into businesses to drive underlying productivity in our economy, and the greater productivity, which is a major challenge for us, is something that, in part, can be aided by increase in capital investment.

The acceleration of that capital investment, even with years and time, while a timing difference in a tax sense, is equally an important point of view from an economic point of view as well. So I don't see that as a negative, bringing forward expenditure, because we get that immediate productivity gain as soon as that asset is deployed. And what we are seeing, and just going to the energy or the climate space just to finish off, is that grid-scale battery operation up that was just opened recently by Contact, again, in public domain,

was a good example of a large-scale capital project which was on the capital plan for out a year. But as a result of the remodeling of the fiscals on investment boost was something that they advised us brought it forward in terms of that capital deployment.

And while that's a capital benefit, it equally has quite significant other economic benefits, such as storage, grid security, and broader implications on energy as well. So, that's the point. If you build a new hydro dam or a new solar farm, you're also going to be eligible for investment boost, and that's something that we are doing to positively incentivize any New Zealand business to deploy capital in a way which helps our economy.
